All right, so with that, a quick recap. Last time we talked about regular expressions or regex, which are useful for filtering and identifying strings that follow some pattern, right? So you have a string and you define a pattern that you want to match, maybe a date that's inside of brackets that looks like this, slash that, slash that and you want to pull information out of a string or you want to only keep strings that match a pattern.
If this is your use case, regex is the perfect tool for you. It's very flexible, very powerful.

This is a big idea. We create a pattern uses a combination of like literal text, what you actually write, and so-called meta characters, right? These basical, basically these special characters that have certain meanings, which we talked about last time. Again, you don't need to memorize exactly what these do, but you should have some familiarity with them and when you see them on a reference sheet or when you look them up, you should remember. Oh, right. Yeah, the dot is the wild card that matches any character except a new line.

When we do it in Python, we use raw strings. There's the re module for doing stuff with strings. Pandas has the dot STR methods in string series that have regular expression methods. Things like find all, extract, extract all, replace and more.

And at the very end I was talking about how regular expressions are sort of sometimes jokingly referred to as a right only language. Right in computing we talk about like read only or like read and write. Regex has had the joke is that it's sort of write only. There's this great quote from the Co creator of whatever came before Firefox said some people when they confronted with a problem think, aha, I'll use a regular expression and then they have two problems.

So they're great for some things. They can be hard to read. It's good to document your regular expression, make it clear what it does in some comments, but certain things that they're not so good at. If you were parsing A hierarchical structure like Jason, use the Jason dot load function. Don't try to use a regular expression. If you're parsing HTML, again, don't try to write a regular expression for that, use an HTML parser.
Actually, there's HTML is actually complex enough that writing a regex to parse it.

Is actually impossible and anything involving counting also not a good use case for regex. So for example if you want to say I want something where it's like a a a a a certain number of times and then B the same number of times that I saw ace in that case, that is something you cannot do with regex, right? Like counting operations like the same number of times. This as that not possible with regex. You have to kind of parse the string yourself.

So regular expressions can sometimes be tasks, can sometimes be done with large language models, right? I can ask, you know, Gemini or ChatGPT or whatever to say, oh, hey, you know, extract the date out of the string.

But a lot of times it's faster, cheaper, and simpler to just write a regular expression instead of adding into my code, Hey, go out, call this LLM. You know, incur the cost of using this many tokens, use up this much water, burn down this many forests, and get me a response, right? So a lot of times a regular expression is more simpler and more powerful and more reliable, right? You know exactly what's going to happen when you use and debug your own regular expression.

So today we're going to talk about plots, right? So we spent a bunch of time on understanding how to like pull stuff out of our data frames, right? We get some table, some data frame, and we want to pull a bunch of stuff out of it, right? We have our, you know, selection, we have groups, we have joins, we have all sorts, all these different things we know how to do with our tables. But we also want to know how do we visualize things? And we've seen some visualization, which have been helpful, but I've kept brushing.
Under the rug. Don't worry about how we make this. I'll talk about it now. Now's the time to actually talk about how we do that.

So today we're going to talk about like the nitty gritty, the nuts and bolts. And next time we're going to expand our discussion of the principles behind good and bad visualizations. We're going to do a little bit of that today. We'll look at some visualizations, critique them, think about how we'd improve them.
That's our visualization sequence for this week. So today we'll start off by talking about the goals of visualization. Like, what's the point of making a visualization?
And this may sound like a kind of an obvious question, but I want to emphasize some things that you may not have thought about much before.

So you know, you've looked at visualizations before in Data 8. You know, we made lots of visualizations, line plots, scatter plots, and even this class. We've done some visualizations already, right? Like histograms and so on.

So what's the point? The point is to provide a high level summary of a complex data set, right? The human eye, the human brain is really, really, really good at finding, identifying and understanding patterns from pictures, right? We are evolved to do it. And so when we see a visualization, it's a great way to like use the power of the human brain that is really good at that, right? It's also a good way of communicating.

Right, when you put a visualization in a report, in a paper, in a talk, in an e-mail, you are communicating an idea, you are communicating some summary to people, right? And it's important to keep in mind what do you want to communicate and how do you want to communicate it?
And how can you communicate it in a way that takes advantage of the fact that whoever is reading it has a pattern recognition, you know, powered brain?

For example, there's this great visualization we use back in Data 8. If you remember the visualization of like actors, we have a number of movies on one axis and like the total amount of money that their movies made on the Y axis. And yes, it's great for seeing patterns, right? There's like maybe kind of a positive correlation. But again, the brain is really good at picking out, hey, there's this one point that's all by itself sitting over there, right? If you remember in Data 8, the punchline was like, yeah, that's the guy who played C3PO in the Star Wars movies, who acted in those 3 movies.
And nothing else.

Right. So our brains are good at not just recognizing patterns, but recognizing exceptions to those patterns, outliers, things that are unusual. What's going on in a visualization that there's no way we could have done just by like looking at a giant wall of text with the table?

So when we think about visualizations, it's not just how do I make a nice pretty picture? Like, yes, you want your visualizations to look nice, but much more important than looking nice is that you want to think hard about this, how you communicate your ideas as effectively as you possibly can. And you need to think hard about how you do that.
So whoops.

Today and on Thursday, we're going to talk about today mostly like choosing the right plot. How do you figure out the right plot for the kinds of data or visualization we want to do? And think about different plots, different variable types, what kind of plots for what, for what variables? And like, we'll talk a little bit about the code you would use to make those plots. And then on Thursday, we're going to talk about thinking things like how do we transform our data so that our plots, and therefore whatever we do downstream of them is going to be more clear.
And we'll think about things like labels and color and other things that improve our the clarity of our plots. But this whole time, the big idea that I want you to keep in the back of your mind.

Is not. How do I make a plot that shows off that I am as smart as I possibly can be? Right. When you make a plot, your goal is not to say, look at me, I'm so smart, I'm so good at making plots. Your goal is to make whoever's looking at them understand the idea you want them to understand with as little thinking as possible. Your viewers are lazy, and you want your plots to work even when your viewers are going to be super lazy.
Right, Because if you think about it, going from data to understanding is not a simple process, right? There's a lot of work involved in going from like data to understanding.

So what you want to do is you want to shoulder most of that burden yourself. You want to say, I'm going to do most of the work of getting from data to understanding and making a visualization that gets my viewer most of the way there. So I'm going to do like this 90 percent, 95% of the work. So then they can look at that visualization and do the remaining 5% and be like, Oh yeah, OK, I get it. It's really simple.
A good visualization will often have your viewer thinking like Oh yeah, the conclusion was totally obvious even if it wasn't.
Right, so you want to make a visualization that does all that work for your viewer?

Right. So how do we do that? You always have to start by thinking, what am I trying to communicate? Because there's no way you can get somebody 95% of the way there if you don't know where they should be going, right? If you don't know what the path is, there's no way you can walk somebody 95% of the way there with a picture.
So that's always the first question we want to have in the back of our heads. What am I trying to communicate with this visualization and how do I communicate it?

All right, so we're going to start by talking about visualizing distributions, which is what we'll send most of today on if we have the time. We'll talk about scatter plots and comparing multiple variables, and we'll also spend some time on something called kernel density estimation.

So what is the distribution? Well, a univariate distribution. Univariate is just a fancy way of saying 1 variable you need. One variant variable is the set of possible values that a variable can take.

And technically, this is a discrete distribution. There are also continuous distributions, which we'll talk about a little more today and you learn a lot more about in the probability class. But essentially a distribution is the set of possible values that a variable can take and the frequency of each one.
So over here I have an example which is majors and.
Count. So I have every possible major that a Cal undergrad can get, and I have a count the number of people who got a degree in that major in 2024.
So this is a distribution. Why is a distribution? Because I have all the possible values these things that the major variable can take on and I have counts of each one.
And if I were to use these counts to calculate percentages or proportions, which I would do by like dividing each one by the total.
Then those percentages would add up to 100, right? Or the proportions would add up to 1?
So if we have something that meets that description, then it is a distribution.
If it doesn't, then it's not.

And again, this is discrete. We'll talk a little bit more about continuous distributions in a SEC. OK, let's get some practice. When we have a variable, what kind of variable we have should inform what kind of visualization we use. So remember this graph, this little diagram I showed you last week of different kinds of variables. So the first thing you need to do is think about what variables you want to visualize.

Some useful sort of quantitative ways of representing a distribution. We have things called quartiles. For a quantitative variable, the first quartile is the 25th percentile, right? Remember, percentile just means that like 25th percentile means 25% of the data are smaller than this amount.
So that's the first quartile or 1/4 of the data are less than this value. Second quartile is two quarters or a half of this data are less than that amount. That's the median.
